name: L-spine layout
description: Floor plan organized around an L-shaped corridor that runs along two perpendicular arms, with rooms strung along the outside of each arm. The longer primary arm carries the principal rooms (bedrooms, parlour, kitchen) while the shorter secondary arm absorbs service or private spaces; the inner corner where the two arms meet becomes the building's natural circulation node and is typically where the main entry or stair lands. Each arm is one corridor-width wide (2-3 blocks) and 6-12 blocks long, giving the whole footprint a characteristic L when seen from above. The pattern suits L-shape mansions, ranch houses, and wings-of-light style residences where every room reaches an exterior wall for daylight and the corridor never feels like a dead tunnel.
placement_rules: Lay out two straight corridor arms meeting at right angles; the longer (primary) arm is 8-12 blocks, the shorter (secondary) arm 6-9 blocks.
Corridor width is constant at 2-3 blocks across both arms so circulation flows continuously around the inner corner without pinch points.
Rooms attach to the outer (convex) edge of each arm so every room reaches at least one exterior wall for windows and daylight.
The inner corner of the L is treated as the central circulation node — main entry, stair landing, or hearth/common area lands here so the two arms read as branches off a single hub.
Distribute 2-4 rooms per arm; primary arm typically carries living/sleeping spaces, secondary arm absorbs service rooms (kitchen, bath, storage) or private wings.
Avoid placing rooms on the inner (concave) side of the L — that space is reserved for the corner node, an open courtyard, or a garden so the L silhouette stays legible from outside.
Single-storey layouts use preferred height 4; two-storey variants extend to 5 with the stair core anchored at the inner corner.

medieval.notes: Stone-and-timber manor wing; corridor lit by torches on cobblestone walls, inner corner often holds a stair to an upper hall or a small hearth alcove.

modern.notes: Ranch-house L with a glazed inner corner overlooking a courtyard or pool; corridor reads as a bright gallery linking the two wings.

victorian.notes: Brick L-plan villa; the inner corner houses the formal entry hall and main stair, with the long arm reserved for parlour and dining and the short arm for service rooms.
